```{r}
#| fig-cap: "Trace plots for the three model parameters. Four chains in different colours. All chains are stationary and overlapping — the hairy caterpillar."

mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "trace",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma")
)
```

::: notes
- Three panels, one per parameter. x-axis = iteration (1 to 1000, post-warmup only). y-axis = parameter value. Four colored lines = four chains.
- **What to look for:**
  1. Stationarity: the mean and spread of each chain should not trend over time.
  2. Mixing: the four chains should be interleaved -- you should not be able to visually identify them by color.
  3. Consistent levels: all chains should explore the same region of parameter space.
- **What to read off the figure:** Intercept chains centered around 2, b_x around 0.7, sigma around 1.
- Connect back to the earlier trace plot comparison: "This looks like the good case from Part VI -- the hairy caterpillar. Not like the separated chains."
- Budget: ~3 minutes.
:::

---

## Posterior Distributions {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: fig-brms-posterior
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| fig-cap: "Posterior distributions for the three model parameters. Shaded region = 95% credible interval. Dashed lines = true data-generating values. All three true values lie clearly inside the 95% credible intervals."

mcmc_plot(
  fit_synth,
  type = "areas",
  variable = c("b_Intercept", "b_x", "sigma"),
  prob = 0.95
) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(2, 0.7, 1),
    linetype = "dashed",
    color = col_2,
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  labs(subtitle = "Dashed lines = true data-generating parameter values")
```

::: notes
- Each panel shows the full posterior density for one parameter.
- **What each element means:**
  - The curve: full posterior density $p(\theta \mid \text{data})$.
  - The shaded region: 95% credible interval. There is 95% posterior probability the true parameter lies in this range.
  - The vertical solid line: posterior median.
  - The orange dashed lines: true parameter values (only possible to show because we generated the data).
- **What to check:** with n = 1000 and weakly informative priors, the posteriors should be narrow, centered very close to the true values, with the dashed lines clearly inside the shaded 95% regions.
- Budget: ~4 minutes.
:::

---

## Posterior Predictive Check {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: fig-pp-check
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| fig-height: 3.0
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive check: 50 datasets simulated from posterior draws (light blue) versus the observed data (dark blue). The observed data distribution should be well within the range of simulated datasets if the model fits well."

pp_check(fit_synth, ndraws = 50)
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Logic:** draw parameter values $\theta^{(s)}$ from the posterior, simulate a new dataset $y^{\text{rep}}$ from $P(y \mid \theta^{(s)})$, compare simulated datasets to the observed data $y$.

If the model is well-specified, observed data should look like a *typical draw* from the posterior predictive distribution. A systematic discrepancy indicates model misspecification.
:::

::: notes
- **Figure:** light blue lines = 50 simulated datasets. Dark blue line = observed data.
- **What to look for:** the dark blue line should be similar to the light blue lines.
- **What success looks like here:** a roughly bell-shaped distribution of $y$ values; the observed distribution (dark blue) falls within the range of simulated distributions (light blue).
- Preview: we will develop more sophisticated checks in weeks 8-13.
- Budget: ~3 minutes.
:::

---

## Reading the Output {.smaller}

Connecting the `brms` output to the MCMC machinery:

. . .

- The **4,000 rows** in the posterior are samples from **4 chains $\times$ 1,000 post-warm-up steps**
- Each step used the **NUTS algorithm** -- a frictionless ball rolling on the negative log-posterior surface -- to propose an accepted move
- **Convergence** was assessed by $\hat{R}$ ($\approx 1.00$ for all parameters), $ESS$ ($> 400$ for all parameters) and visually by trace plots (stationary, mixed chains)
- The **posterior distributions** directly encode our updated beliefs about $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\sigma$ after combining the prior and the likelihood
- $P(\text{data})$ **was never computed**. It cancelled in every acceptance ratio

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.72em"}
When `brms` prints `Compiling Stan program...` and `Chain 1: Iteration 1/2000`, you now know exactly what is happening and why. Next week, the model structure becomes more interesting. The MCMC machinery runs identically underneath.
:::

::: notes
- Closing slide for the brms section. Connect each bullet back to the relevant part of the session.
- "4,000 rows" = Part VI (multiple chains, warmup setup).
- "NUTS algorithm" = Part V (ball on landscape, HMC).
- "Convergence assessed by R-hat" = Part VI (diagnostics, falsificationism).
- "Posterior distributions" = Part IV/V (stationary distribution, Monte Carlo estimator @eq-mc-estimator).
- "P(data) never computed" = Part IV (@eq-pdata-cancels, the cancellation result).
- Budget: ~3 minutes.
:::

# Session Summary

## Learning Goals: Achieved? {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 0.75em;"}
| # | Learning Goal | Key Answer | Where |
|:-:|:-----|:---------|:---:|
| 1 | Why is grid approximation intractable? | Cost grows as $k^d$ (exponential in parameters); 50 params = $10^{150}$ cells | [Part I](#part-i-where-we-left-off) |
| 2 | What is the Monte Carlo idea? | Approximate integrals by averaging over random samples; error $\propto 1/\sqrt{S}$, dimension-free | [Part III](#part-iii-monte-carlo-random-sampling-as-integration) |
| 3 | What is a Markov Chain and its stationary distribution? | A memoryless sequence of states; stationary = the long-run distribution the chain converges to | [Part IV](#part-iv-markov-chains-and-the-metropolis-algorithm) |
| 4 | Why does $P(\text{data})$ cancel? | The acceptance rule uses only a posterior *ratio*; $P(\text{data})$ appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels (@eq-pdata-cancels) | [Part IV](#part-iv-markov-chains-and-the-metropolis-algorithm) |
| 5 | How does HMC differ from Metropolis? | HMC uses gradient of the log-posterior to propose long, physics-guided trajectories; much lower autocorrelation | [Part V](#part-v-the-posterior-as-a-landscape-and-hmc) |
| 6 | How do I interpret `brms` output? | Check $\hat{R} < 1.01$, ESS $> 400$, trace plots for mixing; read Estimate/CI as posterior mean and 95% credible interval | [Part VIII](#part-viii-brms-in-practice) |
| 7 | Why can convergence not be proven? | Diagnostics are tests for non-convergence, not certificates; a chain may pass all checks yet miss a distant mode | [Part VI](#part-vi-multiple-chains-warm-up-and-convergence) |
:::


::: notes
- Return to this slide at the end to close the session loop.
- Walk down the table row by row. Ask students to indicate by a show of hands which goals they feel confident about vs. uncertain about -- this gives you quick formative feedback.
- Goals 4, 6, and 7 are most likely to appear on the take-home exam. Flag this explicitly.
- Budget: ~4 minutes.
:::

# Thank you for your attention and see you next week!

<a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H2a2 2 0 0 1-2-2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a>

::: {#refs}
:::

# Appendix

## Appendix A: Metropolis Algorithm -- Full Pseudocode

:::: columns
::: {.column width="75%"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"}
The complete Metropolis algorithm for Bayesian inference:

**Input:** unnormalized posterior $\tilde{p}(\theta) = P(\text{data} \mid \theta) \cdot P(\theta)$, proposal distribution $q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})$, starting point $\theta^{(1)}$, number of iterations $T$.

**For** $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:

1. **Propose:** draw $\theta^*$ from the proposal distribution $q(\theta^* \mid \theta^{(t)})$

2. **Compute acceptance ratio:**
$$
r = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\tilde{p}(\theta^*)}{\tilde{p}(\theta^{(t)})}\right) = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^*) \cdot P(\theta^*)}{P(\text{data} \mid \theta^{(t)}) \cdot P(\theta^{(t)})}\right)
$$ {#eq-metropolis-full}

3. **Accept or reject:** draw $u \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$
   - If $u \leq r$: set $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^*$ (accept)
   - If $u > r$: set $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)}$ (reject, stay)

**Output:** sequence $\theta^{(1)}, \theta^{(2)}, \ldots, \theta^{(T)}$ -- samples from the stationary distribution $P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ (after warm-up).
:::

:::

::: {.column width="25%"}

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.5em"}
**Why does this work?** The acceptance rule satisfies **detailed balance**: for any two states $\theta$ and $\theta'$, the probability of being in state $\theta$ and moving to $\theta'$ equals the probability of being in $\theta'$ and moving back to $\theta$:
$$
\pi(\theta) \cdot P(\theta \to \theta') = \pi(\theta') \cdot P(\theta' \to \theta)
$$ {#eq-detailed-balance}
Detailed balance implies that $\pi(\theta) = P(\theta \mid \text{data})$ is the stationary distribution. This is the mathematical guarantee that the Metropolis algorithm produces posterior samples.
:::

:::

::::

::: notes
- Appendix slide for students wanting the formal underpinning. Present briefly if time permits; otherwise direct students to it as a reference.
- @eq-metropolis-full: the full algorithm written out as pseudocode with the acceptance ratio made explicit. Point out: the unnormalized posterior $\tilde{p}(\theta)$ is all that is needed -- P(data) has already been dropped.
- @eq-detailed-balance: the mathematical guarantee. Detailed balance is a sufficient condition for a Markov Chain to have a given stationary distribution.
- Informal verification: suppose $p(\theta') > p(\theta)$. Then $P(\theta \to \theta') = q(\theta' \mid \theta) \times 1$ (always accept going uphill). And $P(\theta' \to \theta) = q(\theta \mid \theta') \times p(\theta)/p(\theta')$. With symmetric proposal $q$, detailed balance holds. QED (sketch).
:::

---

## Appendix B: MCMC Algorithm Comparison {.smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 0.65em;"}

| Algorithm | Year | Proposal | Key innovation | Limitation | Used in |
|:---|:---:|:----|:------|:------|:---|
| **Metropolis** | 1953 | Symmetric random walk | First MCMC algorithm; simple acceptance rule | Slow mixing; requires tuning of step size | Historical; rarely used in practice today |
| **Metropolis-Hastings** | 1970 | Asymmetric proposals allowed | Generalises Metropolis to arbitrary proposals | Still requires manual tuning | Textbooks; simple models |
| **Gibbs Sampler** | 1984/1990 | Conditional distributions | Acceptance rate = 1; dimension-by-dimension | Requires tractable conditionals; slow on correlated posteriors | BUGS, JAGS |
| **HMC** | 1987/2011 | Leapfrog trajectory (gradient-guided) | Uses gradient information; large, efficient jumps | Requires differentiable posterior; trajectory length must be tuned | Stan (basic HMC) |
| **NUTS** | 2014 | Adaptive leapfrog (no U-turn) | Eliminates trajectory length tuning; full automation | Computationally expensive per step vs. random walk | **Stan (default) → `brms`** |

:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.68em"}
`brms` uses **NUTS** via Stan for all models. The progression from Metropolis to NUTS represents 60 years of algorithmic development: each generation solved the main limitation of the previous one. NUTS is currently the state of the art for general-purpose Bayesian computation with continuous parameters.

References: @metropolis1953equation; @gelfand1990sampling; @hoffman2014no; @burkner2017brms; @gelman2013bda-ch12.
:::

::: notes
- Reference table for students wanting to situate the session in the broader MCMC literature. Walk through it briefly if time permits.
- **NUTS (2014):** automated HMC. The "no U-turn" criterion detects when the trajectory starts doubling back -- further simulation is wasteful. Stops automatically. This is what Stan implements.
- Historical note: BUGS and JAGS (using Gibbs) dominated before Stan. Stan (2012) represented a generational change in computational efficiency.
:::
